summary: O'Neil explores the implications of facial recognition algorithms that could predict marital success by reading subtle emotional expressions at the altar. She draws a parallel to Huntington's disease genetic testing, where only 5% of at-risk people choose to learn their status. The post questions whether some information is better left unknown, particularly when there's no actionable cure or intervention. O'Neil concludes that for deeply personal matters like love, she'd rather not have algorithmic optimization, even if it were technically possible.
key_points: Facial emotion-reading algorithms combined with research on 'gut feelings' about marriage could theoretically predict relationship outcomes
The value of information depends heavily on whether it's actionable—people avoid knowing bad news when nothing can be done about it
Only 5% of people at risk for Huntington's disease take the genetic test, preferring ambiguity over certainty of bad outcomes
Faith and ambiguity may be essential ingredients in intimate human relationships that optimization would undermine
There's a meaningful distinction between information that helps us and information that merely predicts our fate
money_quotes: Is there some information we are better off not knowing?
I don't think knowing my marriage is likely to be in trouble would help me keep the faith. And every marriage needs a good dose of faith.
For them the value of not knowing that information is larger than knowing.
Until that happens the ambiguity is preferable.
When things get that personal and intimate, I'd rather be left alone, even if an algorithm could help me 'optimize my love life'.
Maybe in 100 years people will treat their computers like love oracles.
key_insight: The power to predict doesn't always translate into the power to help, and some of our most intimate human experiences may require the protection of uncertainty rather than the clarity of algorithmic prediction.
